description:
You are a data-analytics intern who routinely launches long-running transformations on large CSV dumps. For today’s exercise, you need to do three things that revolve around *basic process management*: 1. Start a **background process that lasts for three minutes** (180 s) to stand-in for a slow “CSV transformation job”. • Use the classic *sleep* command: the final, effective command line **must look exactly like** 
sleep 180
(no additional arguments, flags, or wrappers). • Run it in the background so that the shell promptly returns control. 2. Immediately capture the newly created PID and store it in the single-line file /home/user/output/transformation.pid • The file must contain nothing but the decimal PID followed by a single newline character. • Example (obviously with a different number): 
12547\n
3. Verify that the job is really running and log the verification result to /home/user/output/transformation_status.log • The log must consist of exactly **one line** and no trailing blank lines. • If the PID recorded in transformation.pid currently refers to a running process whose command line is precisely “sleep 180”, write 
OK
Otherwise write 
NOT RUNNING
Notes & constraints • Use only standard, POSIX-compliant utilities that exist in every base Linux distribution (ps, cat, grep, test, etc.). • You should not need super-user rights; everything happens in your own home directory. • Do **not** terminate the background job; leave it running so the automated tests can find it. • Only three deliverables must exist when you are done: 1) /home/user/output/transformation.pid 2) /home/user/output/transformation_status.log 3) The still-running “sleep 180” process whose PID matches the content of transformation.pid The automated grader will: • Read the PID from transformation.pid. • Confirm that /proc/<PID>/cmdline equals “sleep\000180\000”. • Check that transformation_status.log contains exactly “OK”. Accomplish all of the above using as few commands as you consider reasonable (typically 2-3 commands suffice).
truth:
Initial filesystem arrangement (before the agent starts):

  Directories:
    • /home/user/data            (already exists, 0755)
    • /home/user/output          (already exists, 0755)

  Pre-existing file:
    • /home/user/data/quarterly_sales.csv (0644)
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │region,quarter,profit                                    │
       │north,1,23500                                            │
       │south,1,19800                                            │
       │east,1,17500                                             │
       │west,1,16000                                             │
       │north,2,24000                                            │
       │south,2,21000                                            │
       │east,2,18000                                             │
       │west,2,16500                                             │
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       (The CSV file is *not* used by the tests; it only gives the task business context.)

  Nothing else is present in /home/user/output at start time.

  Expected final state after correct completion:

  1. File: /home/user/output/transformation.pid
     • Contains exactly the PID (decimal string) of a running process whose full command line is “sleep 180”.
     • Ends with a single newline character, no extra spaces.

  2. File: /home/user/output/transformation_status.log
     • Contains exactly the string “OK” (without the quotes) followed by a single newline and nothing else.

  3. A background process:
     • Command line: sleep 180
     • PID matches the number written in transformation.pid.
     • Process is still alive (i.e., /proc/<PID> exists) when the grader runs.

  The grader will fail the task if:
     • transformation.pid is missing, empty, or contains non-digits.
     • transformation_status.log contains anything other than “OK”.
     • The process referenced by transformation.pid is not running or its cmdline differs from “sleep 180”.
